formal_definition: A crypto exchange traded note (cETN), a debt security referencing the price of one or more cryptoassets. The FCA banned retail sale, marketing and distribution of cETNs (alongside crypto derivatives referencing unregulated transferable cryptoassets) under Policy Statement PS20/10, in force from 6 January 2021; it reopened retail access to cETNs only (the derivatives ban remains in force) from 8 October 2025 via Handbook Notice 132, conditional on the product being admitted to the FCA's Official List and to trading on a UK Recognised Investment Exchange with an FCA-approved prospectus, and sold only by firms with appropriate permissions. The FCA classifies cETNs as Restricted Mass Market Investments (RMMIs): the financial-promotion rules for that category apply in full (risk warnings, appropriateness assessments, Consumer Duty obligations), and investors have no Financial Services Compensation Scheme or Financial Ombudsman Service recourse. A June 2026 consultation (CP26/17) proposes letting UCITS and certain non-UCITS retail schemes hold cETNs directly, capped at 10% of scheme property; this is a proposal, not yet a rule.
plain_definition: A debt security that tracks the price of cryptoassets, traded on an exchange like a bond -- the main way UK retail investors can currently get regulated exposure to crypto price movements, since the UK (unlike Hong Kong) has no spot crypto ETF regime. The FCA lifted its retail ban on these specifically (but not on crypto derivatives) from 8 October 2025, and classifies them as a 'Restricted Mass Market Investment', meaning strict risk warnings apply and there is no compensation-scheme protection.
